questions.question: Why is cache hit rate alone not a sufficient design metric?
options: Because the important question is how much origin QPS, latency, or cost the misses still create.
Because hit rate matters only to frontend teams.
Because caches remove the need for origin capacity planning.
Because miss traffic never affects availability.
questions.explanation: Hit ratio only matters through its impact on the rest of the system.

questions.question: What is the most useful first input when estimating cache benefit?
options: Read QPS on the path the cache protects.
The number of engineers running the service.
The storage retention window.
The number of monitoring dashboards.
questions.explanation: You need the request load the cache is expected to absorb.

questions.question: If a cache protects 90K read QPS with a 92% hit ratio, what is rough origin QPS?
Why can a drop from 92% hit rate to 75% hit rate be dangerous even if both numbers sound high?
options: Because the origin miss load can increase by several times, not just by the difference in percentage points.
Because caches stop serving any traffic below 80%.
Because lower hit rate changes only storage cost.
Because hit rate does not affect downstream systems.
questions.explanation: Miss traffic is the nonlinear part that hits the origin.

questions.question: Which workload pattern most often makes a single fleet-wide hit rate misleading?
options: Highly skewed hot objects and a long cold tail
A service with only one API route
Uniformly tiny payloads
A single-region deployment
questions.explanation: Different object classes can hide very different miss behavior.

questions.question: Which observability metric best validates whether cache is protecting origin as intended?
options: Origin QPS attributable to misses, broken down by route or object class
Number of cache clusters provisioned
Monthly retention size
Count of successful deploys
questions.explanation: Direct miss-attributed origin load is the signal you care about.

questions.question: What trade-off usually comes with aggressively optimizing hit rate?
options: Lower origin load, but more invalidation, warming, and freshness complexity.
Lower write durability, but higher read consistency.
Lower latency with no operational downside.
Higher CPU use only in the database layer.
questions.explanation: Higher hit rate often requires more sophisticated cache management.

questions.question: When is miss latency especially important to estimate separately from miss QPS?
options: When the cache primarily protects user-visible tail latency, not just origin capacity.
When the service has no users.
When retention is shorter than 30 days.
When all requests are writes.
questions.explanation: Miss cost can be latency pain even when origin capacity is still acceptable.
